guest_role: VP of Product and Growth at Uber (2013-2017), head of international growth at Facebook, angel investor
assertiveness_why: Harry asks detailed follow-up questions and occasionally references external frameworks (Sarah Tavell's hype subsidy critique) to probe thinking, but remains primarily in collaborative, learning-focused mode without sustained challenge.
question_style: Exploratory, asking for examples and stories to illustrate frameworks; occasionally probes assumptions but mostly receiving; conversational requests like 'I feel terrible just keeping you on asking for examples.'
tone: Warm, collaborative learning posture; Harry repeatedly thanks Ed for examples and shows genuine interest in frameworks; professional but personable with light self-deprecation when asking for repeated examples.
depth: Technical deep dive into growth fundamentals (North Star metrics, K-factor, viral loops, product-market fit), growth team structure (five functional areas: product, design, engineering, marketing, data), international expansion dynamics (Japan invite→announcement cultural adaptation), two-sided marketplace constraints (supply vs demand), CEO-growth team relationships, A-B testing discipline, hiring criteria for growth leaders, and investment thesis.
harry_quotes: What were the key lessons from your time at Facebook?
How do you advise growth leaders to determine needle movers versus nice to haves?
What would the North Star metric be at Facebook and Uber?
When's the right time for founding teams to start thinking about growth?
How should I literally structure the process of hiring a head of growth?
learnings: Product-market fit (organic user love, no CAC required) must precede growth team formation; premature growth hiring wastes resources on companies drilling in the wrong place.
North Star metrics must encapsulate entire business value (weekly trips = riders + drivers at Uber, MAU = all users at Facebook); different business models require different North Stars.

Needle movers are the 1-2 interventions per year driving exponential improvement; 90% of A-B tests yield micro-optimizations with negligible impact (e.g., changing button color vs adding rider-to-driver conversion button).
CEO involvement in growth (weekly deep-dive meetings, getting into the weeds) creates cultural resonance and validates team work; becomes unfeasible at scale but drives early-stage morale.
Organizational friction between growth and product grows with scale; mitigated by weekly sync meetings between leaders and 6-monthly shared roadmap planning to prevent overlap.
International expansion requires cultural adaptation, not template rollout (Japan: renaming invites to announcements increased adoption 10x by removing cultural shame).
Two-sided marketplace growth focuses on supply-constrained side; small UI changes (rider app button suggesting driver sign-up) can match all paid marketing spend combined.
Hiring experienced growth leaders requires assessing past experiments, challenge problems, and people management via 360 feedback; avoid leaders strong on metrics but weak on team building.
notable: In-depth guide to growth team structure and hiring; Ed shares specific examples including secret India credit-card-removal test that doubled conversion and contradicted CEO's directive (CEO validated data afterward); discusses five functional areas of growth teams and the challenge of managing product-growth friction at scale.
